g.data-narration: Welcome to this comprehensive guide on the Global Context of E-Waste. Let's explore this critical environmental challenge.
g.text: Understanding the worldwide e-waste challenge and management strategies

g.data-narration: E-waste includes discarded electronics like computers, phones, and appliances that have reached their end-of-life stage.
g.text: Electronic waste or e-waste refers to discarded electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) that has reached
its end-of-life stage. This includes computers, mobile phones, televisions, refrigerators, and
various consumer electronics. The rapid advancement of technology and increasing consumption
of electronic devices have made e-waste one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally.

g.data-narration: Global e-waste reached 53.6 million tonnes in 2019, projected to grow 40% to 74.7 million tonnes by 2030.
Asia leads global e-waste generation with 45%, followed by Americas 27%, Europe 20%, and Africa 7%.
E-waste contains over 1000 substances including hazardous materials like lead and mercury, plus valuable resources like gold and copper.
g.text: Causing environmental pollution and health risks
Gold, Silver, Copper, Palladium, Rare Earth Elements
Potential for resource recovery and circular economy

g.data-narration: The Basel Convention regulates e-waste movement across borders, requiring prior informed consent for hazardous waste exports.
EPR shifts end-of-life responsibility to producers, incentivizing eco-design. The EU's WEEE Directive mandates collection and recycling targets.
g.text: Policy principle that makes producers responsible for end-of-life management of their products,
including collection and recycling. This shifts burden from municipalities/consumers to producers.
🇮🇳 India E-Waste Rules 2016: Producer responsibility via PROs

g.data-narration: India's E-Waste Rules 2016 mandate producers collect and channelize e-waste through Producer Responsibility Organizations.
g.text: Manages e-waste collection channelization for producers

g.data-narration: Approximately 100,000 workers in India engage in informal e-waste recycling using primitive methods, causing severe health and environmental hazards.
g.text: ⚠️ Informal E-Waste Recycling Sector in India
Major hubs: Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata

g.data-narration: Only 17.4% of global e-waste is formally collected and recycled, meaning over 44 million tonnes are landfilled or informally processed.
Germany implements the WEEE Directive through ElektroG, achieving over 65% collection rate with dual collection systems.
g.text: 🇪🇺 Case Study: E-Waste Management in European Union
• Minimum 65% collection rate of average WEEE placed on market
• Producers finance collection, treatment, and recycling

g.data-narration: Ghana's Agbogbloshie demonstrates environmental injustice, receiving e-waste from developed nations and causing toxic pollution.
g.text: ⚠️ Case Study: E-Waste in Ghana (Environmental Justice)
✋ Highlights environmental justice concerns - developed nations' consumption creates burden for developing countries
